item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Behind the scenes on one of my favorite things I've ever written! My thought process here is the same as the one when writing the article, but I was younger when I wrote this and therefore it's not as refined as it could be.

The dates of the interview logs and her age are purposefully blacked out. By the end of this article, Carly (2118) is around 9 years old; however, she is younger in the very first interview. In addition, in interview #1 she is upset because she's been taken to a new place and is regressing a little bit, which is common for children in traumatic situations. By the time the third interview rolls around, she's 9, and she's been surrounded by nothing but adult scientists for Gd knows how long. Lack of interaction with other children will alter social skills and manner of speaking significantly and can result in children that adults charitably call "old souls" when really they're traumatized kids stunted in emotional growth. Carly hasn't been allowed to be a child, and to me, there's not a lot more heartbreaking than a child who acts like an adult because they know no other way.

Source: I work with underprivileged and/or traumatized kids professionally (which is probably why so many of my SCPs involve kids)! It's a depressing but incredibly rewarding line of work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great job, definitely upvoted, but if I have any constructive criticism is that SCP-2118's tone of speaking (or in this case, signing) seems a little inconsistent. She sounds like a scared little kid in the first log, and in the last log she sounds like a bitter postpubescent individual who is 100% depressingly used to her anomaly. I know her age isn't given, and I'm definitely not saying she should sound like a little kid the whole time, as most people overestimate how "kiddy" real children sound when writing for children. I'm just saying she doesn't really feel like the same person by the end…. maybe making her sound more grim and expecting of her fate with the foundation in the beginning, or a little less introspective at the end. I'd prefer the former, as again, IRL kids aren't as naive as people think, especially those who have already been through trauma in their lives.

Still, really good article!

item.title: I like it
item.content:encoded:
Sad and beautiful. I think the author did a good job.
P.S. I kinda get a jerky vibe from Wu, at least as far as 2118 is concerned. She deserves better!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s really because of cannon. There is none. The Foundation can care or be as deprived as you want them to be, it’s all up to personal preference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the concept and, for the most part, the execution of this SCP. however, one thing that annoys me about this and other human SCPs is that the Foundation unnecessarily and randomly dehumanizes SCPs to varying degrees. For example, interviewers asked Iris (SCP-105) to state her name in interviews. However, for no apparent reason, doctors attempt to strip Stella (SCP-134) and this SCP of their names and human identities. They seem to be deprived, to varying degrees, of human emotional necessities. Meanwhile, SCP-999, a possibly-sentient blob of jelly, is expressly allowed to be cuddled and played with in the containment procedure documentation. When the foundation decides to be inhumane, there needs to be a logical reason or at least a better narrative reason than to elicit pity from the reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea of a small child being capable of mimicing the voice of deceased children gives it a small sense of concern if in the point of view of a parent, but perhaps try adding more to the interviews for more depth. I can imagine how painful it must be to hear your dead kid through an scp that mimics and others like 939 make it somewhat bone chilling if ever a breach. I was also wondering if she is actually channeling the voices of deceased or just simply playing with people's heads when in contact with said parent. A simple channel of a deceased person does not strike as much fear as intentionally toying with one's head, but if the child is being forced to channel then it gives a sense of sadness along with fear or worry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm definitely not crying. Definitely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last interview makes the article for me though after a moment, I wonder.
How is it that "Dewey" became aware of what Anselman does as his job?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor kid

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: I'd read that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this, a cool idea that was written very well. Especially those interviews. Though I'm getting the feeling that people employed by the Foundation are just really shitty parents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The G.O.I. would never use an anomalous agent. Their goal is to destroy all SCP's. They would've killed 2118 without considering using her.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think 2118 is classified as Safe because her effect isn't inherently dangerous, except for someone like Anselman, who went out of their way to try and make 2118 into their kid. She's safe because the chances of her straight up seriously injuring and/or killing someone are extremely low.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Great. Now I'm imagining a tea party with a bunch of little girls in Level C HAZMAT gear.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Can SCP-734 hang out with them? I bet he's old enough now.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
But 191 is too busy hanging out with SCP-2273 to chill with those guys!

Congrats on your +100, by the way.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: I just want a world where 2118, SCP-134, SCP-191, and SCP-1338 can all hang out and be buddies.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
Well deserved!

I really enjoy this one. I keep imagining an unlikely friendship between her and SCP-134.
Whoever upvoted it didn't leave a comment, so I'm doing it, because dammit I deserve it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's true ASL. My partner is HoH and I've worked with Deaf actors and translators before. The way I've written it now is an artistic choice that I've cleared with other Deaf people and users of ASL. I considered adding another clarification in there but felt it bogged down the document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I've had a good read through this and just have a few questions.

It states that 2118 uses ASL but doesn't say whether this is true ASL or SEE (Signed Exact English) or even SSE (Sign Supported English). As such, there's a little bit of an issue with the interviews if they're in ASL. I studied BSL and Deaf Culture at university as well as a bit of ASL, so I was just wondering if you could clarify which method of sign 2118 is using so I could provide some tips on writing down translated sign language?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I specify ASL later, but I agree, it should be done earlier as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only complaint here is that ASL is not the only/default "sign language", so the containment procedures ought to specify ASL.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (Is there a way to delete all my comments?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh hello, goosebumps, old friends! It's been so long, really, too long! +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. A good question that I didn't think about because in other SCPs they shouldn't be restrained, but here he absolutely should've. Fixed.
  2. Agents M██████████ and C█████ were called in, so I think it's implied they have proper clearance. EDIT: Oh, you were probably talking about Anselman. He's not in her cell, he's outside of it. I didn't want to get pedantic about the layout of this wing of whatever site she's in, but in my head it's laid out like the prison in Silence of the Lambs, but every cell is like Hannibal's. Glass, with air/communication holes.
  3. Agent M██████████ either a) killed her son a long time ago and covered it up very well or b) killed her son that morning and no one's found the body yet. Even the best of people kill their kids. It's terrible and it happens. (Also, why would the Foundation use D-Class as agents?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As for your last question, it's implied that this particular agent somehow managed to conceal that part of his nature (until his exposure to the SCP).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why was the D-Class unrestrained for a test that was likely to cause them to damage the girl? Why are people with improper clearance able to get into her room without triggering a breach alarm? Why is the Foundation using child-killers as agents instead of D-Class?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm impressed with this article. This is how you elicit a emotional response in a natural way. You presented the scenario and let it unfold organically. At no point did I feel that you were trying to force sadness out of me in a hamfisted manner. It just.. flowed. I swear too many writers on this site think they have to take their metaphorical sledgehammers out and bludgeon the readers upside the head with feels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really nice one. That last interview… now that's how you make an SCP that makes people go insane.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's from Flickr and I remember it being under fair use, but it seems I've misplaced its actual URL. In accordance I've changed the picture to something I can actually attribute. My first post is updated accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: important question: where did you get this photo ? Is it yours? Did you find it somewhere online?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for the interview logs. very good with the balance between creepy and sad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, that's something I keep going back and forth on.

Spoilers
– hide block

At first she was a medium, and if the kid died violently they could only remember their last words, but if they died peacefully they could communicate a little bit. About halfway through I changed her to an echo chamber. She really can't control it, and the scar is there because of it. The scar is meant to imply that she did it to a stranger who struck her hard enough to make the scar. After that, I don't think she'd do it to anyone if she could control it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whenever in the presence of someone with a dead child it copies what they were saying as they died and is genuinely freaked the hell out by it. The child doesn't want to do this, but has to because it can't control its anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is very well-done, a solid idea. It maintains a clinical tone throughout, and has great grammar, spelling, and content. However, I could see how some people might become confused by it. Is the child being forced to mimic the dead kids, and can't control its anomalous effect, or is the child simply doing it, then faking an apology? A bit more clarification on this would be lovely.
Other than that, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But that could use improvement too. Never had children and is versed in sign language and translation etiquette and usage would work better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right you are, made those adjustments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, glad to help out with explaining why all the extra stuff just makes this SCP that much more awesome. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's what I was going for. I originally only had the second interview and was accused of being too obvious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done! Pardon the oversight. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your opinion is valid, of course, but I think it's a little presumptuous to think that you speak for the majority of the audience. Can you offer any specific places where you think its a bit much?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even if the first two aren't necessary I thought they still added a bit to the SCP as a whole. Kind of like…those books where each chapter starts with a quote from a famous writer or artist or political figure. They help build suspense for the chapter you're about to read or the end of the book itself. Sure, the SCP would work without the first two interviews, but they make it more interesting by being there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Point taken. It's nice having a reasonable argument with someone who doesn't yell just because for every answer. You're pretty cool Live2Die. This was fun. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last interview log. Man.

Also, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My primary qualms are as follows:

I don't like seeing stories wherein Foundation personnel are rapists/murderers/otherwise severely morally compromised. I know that Foundation personnel aren't perfect, stoic heroes or whatever, but I do believe the Foundation would generally be able to pick up on that shit before it bit them in the ass.

I don't think the first two interviews are necessary; you drop them, you get the exact same result, but less time to get there.

DISCLAIMER: I'm very, very, vet far from being the best writer on this site, so my concrit weighs a bit less.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That might happen, but it's extremely unlikely. Besides, there are enough SCPs with a ~twist~ that makes it special like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry about that. I do use punctuation, but that one kind of turned into a run on sentence. Again, sorry.

Also, that's my point. The Foundation thinks they know everything these SCP's can do, but eventually one will show up and stay in containment for years without ever causing any problems, then it'll get the signal to unleash some power they never knew it had and tear the site where they have it apart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They don't have lax containment procedures; it just doesn't need any more because they studied it and know exactly what it can do.
As a side note: I can only speak for myself here, but it's pretty hard to read your posts, so can you please learn how to use punctuation? It would make it a lot easier to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess so, but I maintain that all humans should be Euclid on the grounds that one day they might get a "safe" human who was a sleeper agent for a G.O.I or something then when everyone thinks its just a harmless kid or depressed clown it goes nuts with some new power they never knew about and causes a massive containment breach because of its own lax containment procedures due to its safe class. Just saying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You need to name this one, and to add that name to the List.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree the description needs some work, but I really like the interviews, not to mention the fact that the girl is (for once) aware of what's happening and distressed by it. That's a very good angle to have on something like this.

item.title: Small Edit
item.content:encoded: i changed "time" in Special Containment Procedures to "times" for flow/ grammar reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My god, that's horrible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, no. If it's easy to contain, it's safe, which SCP-1799 is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I thought the safe class on that one was a little lax.
One guy cracked his skull open because of 1799 and no one can hear him talk without succumbing to his effects. I know that should be in the 1799 discussion page, but my point is that humans generally should be Euclid or higher. Not safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, that's not something that's written in stone.
I've seen a lot of Safe humanoids with a similar level of intelligence. SCP-1799 comes to mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hits a nice mixture of sad and creepy that works quite well for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll +1 it because it's a neat idea. But I thought sentient objects are automatically classified as Euclid?
